summary: O'Neil draws a parallel between failed measurement proxies and capitalism's use of profit as a stand-in for human value. She argues that just as proxies in other domains become gamed and disconnected from their original purpose, profit has become detached from genuine societal benefit. The financial system exemplifies this failure, with its complexity enabling manufactured externalities that regulators cannot address. She concludes by reflecting on her own desire to create something valuable rather than merely profitable, refusing to measure progress with the wrong metric.
key_points: Profit functions as a proxy for human value, subject to the same failure modes as other proxies: initial awareness of limitations fades while gaming increases
The concept of externalities in economics represents an attempt to grapple with the gap between money and actual value
The financial system's complexity and technological sophistication make it particularly susceptible to exploiting the difference between value and profit
Individuals internalize the profit proxy, judging human worth by wealth and conflating high pay with high value
Regulation exists partly to address externalities but cannot keep pace with manufactured ones in complex systems
money_quotes: When an individual loses sight of the failures of the profit proxy, they value themselves or others in terms of how much money they have or the rate at which they get paid.
They infer that if someone is highly paid or rich, they must be valuable. If someone's poor, they must hold no value.
Someone could probably write a book reframing large parts of the financial system as purely devoted to exploiting the difference between value and money.
Most of the time they're not even aware of the value being extracted from them, or are helpless to confront it adequately.
I'm hoping to create something valuable, rather than merely profitable. I don't want to avoid profit, obviously, but I don't want to measure my progress with the wrong stick.
key_insight: Capitalism's reliance on profit as a proxy for value follows the same trajectory as other failed proxies: initial utility gives way to gaming and disconnection from what we actually care about measuring.
